entry.title: SAP DRC EDI / IDOC Support
entry.content:
Does anyone have experience with the established EDI process in relation to electronic invoicing in Germany?

Many customers exchange outgoing invoices via EDI. This includes, amongst other things, the intercompany process in SAP, where an outgoing invoice can be automatically posted as an incoming invoice in the requesting company code via an IDoc.

According to the law, it is stated that this electronic procedure may continue to be used as long as the format is interoperable with EN 16931 and all the necessary information can be extracted. But what exactly does that mean? How should interoperability be achieved? Are there already functions for this in SAP DRC?

Many thanks
Best regards
Maurice

entry.title: Re: FBL1N - Order Number selection criterion no longer fi...
entry.content:
Hi Raymond,
 
Thank you for the suggested notes.
 
I reviewed both SAP Note 310886 and KBA 3455167. As far as I understand, they mainly address issues related to Dynamic Selections and custom fields added through the logical database (KDF) using transaction SE36.
 
In our case, the issue seems slightly different:
- The "Order Number" field is already available in FBL1N.
- The value can be entered without any issue.
- However, in S/4HANA the report output is not filtered according to the entered value.
- Using the same selection criteria in an ECC system, the filtering works as expected.
 
Therefore, the problem does not appear to be related to missing dynamic selection fields or SE36 customization, but rather to the way the selection criterion is processed during report execution in S/4HANA.
 
I will continue investigating and may raise an SAP support case to determine whether this is an intended S/4HANA behavior, a known issue, or a missing correction.
 
Thank you again for your help and suggestions.
 
Best regards,
Florent

entry.title: Re: SAP S/4HANA BP screen enhancement with custom tab
entry.content:
I had exactly the same issue. Since I couldn't find many posts describing this specific situation, I wanted to share the solution here in case it helps someone else in the future. This thread was extremely helpful in pointing me in the right direction.

After some debugging, I discovered that the problem was not in the BDT implementation itself, but in the CVI extension structures.

Initially, I had extended the following structures:

CVIS_EI_EXTERN
  -> CUSTOMER
    -> CENTRAL_DATA
      -> CENTRAL
        -> DATA
        -> DATAX

However, my custom fields were appended to KNVV (Sales Area Data). For KNVV fields, this is not the correct location.

The custom fields must instead be added to:

CVIS_EI_EXTERN
  -> CUSTOMER
    -> SALES_DATA
      -> SALES
        -> DATA
        -> DATAX

Once I added my Z-fields to both the DATA and DATAX structures under SALES_DATA, the values were correctly transferred by the CVI framework and persisted in the KNVV table during BP save.

So, if your custom KNVV fields are displayed correctly in the BP screen but are not saved to the database, double-check that you have extended the Sales Data structures instead of the Central Data structures. This small detail was the root cause of my issue.

Hopefully this saves someone else a few hours of debugging!

entry.title: Re: SAP QM - How to Perform Partial Reversal of Usage Dec...
entry.content:
When you complete a UD, the system posts the entire accepted/rejected quantity from inspection stock to the target stock type (e.g., 321 → unrestricted). SAP standard supports complete reversal of the UD posting (movement type 322), but not partial reversal. The reversal is tied to the inspection lot status. Once UD is made, the lot is considered closed, and SAP does not allow you to “re‑open” it for partial corrections.

Hence, Partial reversal is possible via BAPI_GOODSMVT_CREATE with 322, but the inspection lot quantities/status will not be updated. Best practice is to treat the partial reversal as a stock correction, not a QM correction. The UD remains valid, and you avoid touching QM tables directly.

entry.title: The Missing Web Search: Bolting Brave onto an OpenClaw Agent
entry.content:
The Missing Web Search: Bolting Brave onto an OpenClaw Agent

As of 2026-07-10. Config snippets and CLI versions reflect the state of OpenClaw and the bx CLI at time of writing. TOOLS.md excerpts contain internal timestamps (2026-07-07/08) reflecting when that config was written — not the post date.

A small OpenClaw story about a real gap in the default tool lineup, and the surprisingly durable workaround: drop a binary on persistent storage, prepend $PATH, and let the agent shell out.

TL;DR

The problem:

  • My LLM agent framework of choice (OpenClaw) ships 13 web-search provider integrations.
  • Twelve of them need an API key.
  • The one that doesn't — DuckDuckGo — is currently bot-detection-blocked for programmatic use, so web_search returns empty results or 403s in practice.
  • And routing Brave / Tavily / Exa / etc. through the LiteLLM proxy in front of SAP AI Core isn't the answer either — search APIs authenticate per-caller, not per-model-request. That's a structural limitation of every LLM proxy, not an OpenClaw quirk.

The solution:

  • Install the Brave Search CLI (bx) as a static binary into your agent's workspace, add that path to tools.exec.pathPrepend, and let the agent invoke it via exec.
  • Tell the agent about it in TOOLS.md.
  • ✅Survives container rebuilds (the workspace is a persistent volume).
  • ✅Works with any model — the search key never touches your LLM proxy.
  • ✅One binary drop, one config line, one TOOLS.md note. ~20 minutes end-to-end.
  • ✅Brave's free tier includes $5/month in credits — light agent usage fits entirely inside that.

Note: If you run an OpenClaw agent behind LiteLLM (or any other model proxy) and don't want to plumb search-API keys into your model provider, this is the pattern.

The gap

Here's the tools block from my live agent config, verbatim:

tools:
  web:
    search:
      enabled: true
      provider: duckduckgo
      maxResults: 10
      timeoutSeconds: 30
      cacheTtlMinutes: 15
  exec:
    pathPrepend:
      - /home/node/.openclaw/workspace/bin
 

✅Eight lines. Two moving parts. That's the whole story of this post.

The tools.web.search block enables the built-in web_search tool the agent sees. Provider duckduckgo was chosen for one reason: it doesn't require an API key. Everything else in the search provider lineup does:

 

ProviderShips asNeeds key?
DuckDuckGoduckduckgo-searchNo (public HTML scrape)
Bravebrave-searchYes
TavilytavilyYes
Exaexa-searchYes
SearXNGsearxng-searchDepends on your instance
Perplexityperplexity-searchYes
Geminigemini-searchYes (Google AI)
Grokgrok-searchYes (xAI)
Kimikimi-searchYes
MiniMaxminimax-searchYes
Ollamaollama-searchLocal runtime
Parallelparallel-searchYes
FirecrawlfirecrawlYes

For a proof-of-concept in a fresh workspace, DuckDuckGo is the frictionless choice. In production, it's the only keyless option — and it turns out to be unreliable enough that I stopped using it.

Why not just add a search key to the LiteLLM config?

The obvious question if you already run LiteLLM in front of SAP AI Core: can't I plumb a Brave/Tavily/Exa key through the same proxy that fronts my models?

Short answer: no.

  • LiteLLM (and every other model proxy — LangSmith proxy, LiteLLM Gateway, custom internal ones) is a model proxy.
  • It translates OpenAI-compatible chat/completions calls to whatever backend serves them.
  • It has no concept of "search tools" as first-class routable endpoints, because search APIs aren't a chat/completions verb — they're separate, per-caller-authenticated services.

You can, in theory, wrap a search API in a custom proxy plugin. But now you're maintaining a fork, and the proxy is doing something outside its job description. Not worth it for one CLI-shaped problem.

The other alternative is model-native web search — the search happens inside the model's tenancy, on the provider's side.

It works, but:

  • It only works for a subset of models (not the Claude tiers I route through SAP AI Core).
  • Results and cost are opaque.
  • You lose the ability to grep, cache, filter, or otherwise inspect raw search results.

I wanted a search path that:

  • Works with any model I route through LiteLLM.
  • Costs money against a search vendor I chose, on a bill I control.
  • Returns structured JSON I can pipe through jq and reason over.

That means: a CLI, invoked via exec, using a search-vendor API key held in a workspace env var.

web_search vs. web_fetch — don't confuse them

Two built-in tools that are easy to mix up:

 

ToolInputOutputWhen you'd use it
web_searchA query stringRanked list of results (titles, URLs, snippets) — no full page contentYou don't know which page has the answer; you want candidates.
web_fetchA specific URLReadable text extracted from that one pageYou already have the URL; you want its content.

web_fetch is not affected by the DuckDuckGo problem — it's a URL-to-content extractor and it works fine. But it can't find pages for you. If your agent needs to discover a URL first, it needs web_search — exactly the tool that's broken.

This is also why bx context is a nice primitive: it does both jobs in a single API call. Search + extract, one round-trip, one budget.

In the built-in tools that would be web_search → pick a URL → web_fetch (two round-trips), assuming web_search returned anything useful.

DuckDuckGo: why the default failed in practice

OpenClaw's duckduckgo-search provider is a lightweight HTML scrape of duckduckgo.com.

It works well right up until DuckDuckGo decides you look like a bot — which happens fast when an autonomous agent fires three queries a minute from a datacenter IP.

Symptoms:

  • Empty results: [] arrays for queries that clearly have results in a browser.
  • Occasional HTTP 403 with anti-abuse messaging in the body.
  • Intermittent "success" responses containing a CAPTCHA challenge as the first "result."

None of this is a bug in OpenClaw. It's the reality of scraping a search UI that doesn't want to be scraped.

The DuckDuckGo Instant Answer API is a real API but returns almost nothing useful for open-ended queries — it's designed for unit conversions and Wikipedia summaries.

⚠️ The worst kind of tool is one that looks like it works. Silent failure at the tool layer produces silent hallucination at the model layer — the agent confidently reports "no results found" when in fact the search never ran.

The workspace-binary pattern

A little context on where this agent runs:

OpenClaw is deployed on the same Kubernetes cluster as the diagnostic agent from the companion post. It runs as a long-lived sandbox (a pod) with a PersistentVolumeClaim mounted at /home/node/.openclaw/workspace/. Model traffic goes through the same LiteLLM proxy that fronts SAP AI Core — Claude Sonnet/Opus tiers, Nova, embeddings — all on the sap/* catalog.

Everything under ~/.openclaw/workspace/ is durable state. Everything outside it (system packages, /tmp, npm globals) is ephemeral.

Combine that with tools.exec.pathPrepend, which injects arbitrary directories at the front of the agent's shell PATH, and you get a durable tool-extension pattern:

  1. Drop a static binary into ~/.openclaw/workspace/bin/.
  2. Add that directory to tools.exec.pathPrepend.
  3. Tell the agent (in TOOLS.md) when to reach for it.

No skill packaging, no plugin manifest, no framework code change.

  • ✅Survives container rebuilds.
  • ✅No custom operator, no sidecar.
  • ✅Hot-reloads — no agent restart.

Here's the current state on my workspace:

$ ls -la /home/node/.openclaw/workspace/bin/
-rwxrwxr-x 1 node node 2110240 Jul  7 18:15 bx

$ /home/node/.openclaw/workspace/bin/bx --version
bx 1.5.0
 

2.1 MB, a single self-contained Rust binary from the Brave team. Sitting there since 2026-07-07, survived several pod restarts.

The one-time config edit:

tools:
  exec:
    pathPrepend:
      - /home/node/.openclaw/workspace/bin
 

That is the entire mechanical change. Hot-reloads, no restart required.

This pattern isn't Brave-specific. Anything that ships as a static binary, needs an API key you don't want in your LLM proxy, and returns useful text/JSON on stdout is a candidate: yt-dlp, ripgrep, a custom internal CLI, whatever.

Installing bx durably

⚠️ Install into the persistent workspace, not the container's global path. Otherwise the binary vanishes on the next pod restart.

Option A — one-off manual install (from inside the agent sandbox or via exec😞

mkdir -p /home/node/.openclaw/workspace/bin
cd /home/node/.openclaw/workspace/bin

BX_VERSION=1.5.0
curl -sSL \
  "https://github.com/brave/brave-search-cli/releases/download/v${BX_VERSION}/bx-linux-x86_64" \
  -o bx
chmod +x bx

# Configure the API key once:
./bx config set-key "$BRAVE_SEARCH_API_KEY"

# Sanity check:
./bx --version
./bx web "kubernetes ingress rewrite" | head -20
 

Option B — Kubernetes initContainer (production-grade, automatic on pod start, arch-aware):

- name: bx-install
  image: curlimages/curl:latest
  command:
    - /bin/sh
    - -c
    - |
      mkdir -p /workspace/bin
      if [ ! -f /workspace/bin/bx ]; then
        ARCH=$(uname -m)
        [ "$ARCH" = "aarch64" ] && BX_ARCH="linux-arm64" || BX_ARCH="linux-amd64"
        curl -sL \
          "https://github.com/brave/brave-search-cli/releases/download/v1.5.0/bx-1.5.0-$BX_ARCH" \
          -o /workspace/bin/bx \
          && chmod 0755 /workspace/bin/bx \
          && echo "bx installed ($ARCH)" \
          || echo "bx install failed"
      else
        echo "bx already present: $(/workspace/bin/bx --version 2>/dev/null || echo unknown)"
      fi
  volumeMounts:
    - mountPath: /workspace
      name: workspaces-pvc
 

Option B is what we use in production. The if [ ! -f ... ] check makes it idempotent — on warm restarts it logs the version and exits immediately. BRAVE_SEARCH_API_KEY is injected from a Kubernetes Secret via envFrom.

💡 bx "query" (bare invocation) is an alias for bx context — both call the RAG-optimised extraction endpoint. The distinction matters when you're writing TOOLS.md, because the subcommand name signals intent to the agent.

The honest part: the agent doesn't discover this on its own

The model doesn't spontaneously pick bx over web_search. From the agent's perspective, web_search is a first-class, well-documented tool in the system prompt. bx is just an executable on $PATH, indistinguishable from curl or awk. The agent has no way to know that web_search returns garbage unless you tell it.

In OpenClaw, you tell it via TOOLS.md — a workspace-local file the agent is instructed to read for environment-specific tool notes. Here's the relevant section from mine:

## Web Search

**Preferred: `bx` (Brave Search CLI)** — the configured `web_search`
tool routes through DuckDuckGo which is currently bot-detection-blocked,
so use the `bx` CLI directly instead.

- Binary: `/home/node/.openclaw/workspace/bin/bx`
  (also on PATH via `tools.exec.pathPrepend`)
- API key: auto-loads from `BRAVE_SEARCH_API_KEY` env var
- Docs: https://brave.com/search/api/guides/use-with-openclaw/

**Which subcommand to pick:**
- `bx "query"` or `bx context "query"` → **default for RAG grounding**,
  pre-extracted clean text, token-budgeted. Best when reasoning over content.
- `bx answers "query"` → AI-synthesized answer with citations.
  ⚠️ **Not on current plan** — returns 400 `OPTION_NOT_IN_PLAN`.
  Don't call.
- `bx web "query"` → raw web results (titles/URLs/descriptions).
  Best for finding sources or scanning multiple.
- `bx news "query" --freshness pd|pw|pm|py` → news with time filter.
- `bx images`, `bx videos`, `bx places` → specialised.

**Example (raw web + jq):**
    bx web "world cup 2026 quarter finals" | \
      jq -r '.web.results[] | "\(.title)\n  \(.url)\n"'
 

Two things worth calling out:

It documents a plan-specific gotcha. bx answers returns OPTION_NOT_IN_PLAN on my current Brave tier. Without the note, the agent would call it, see the 400, and either loop or fall back to something worse. With the note, the agent skips it entirely. This is exactly the kind of environment-specific state that belongs in TOOLS.md, not in a shared skill.

It gives the agent a decision matrix. bx context and bx web return different shapes — context is pre-extracted clean text (perfect for feeding to a reasoning model), web is raw ranked results (perfect for scanning URLs). Documenting both means the agent doesn't have to guess. Writing just "Preferred: bx" would have it pick one subcommand and stick with it forever.

📝Your agent framework probably has an equivalent to TOOLS.md — whatever it's called (AGENTS.md, .claude.md, a system-prompt fragment, a project-specific tool doc), use it. This is where operational reality lives.

What bx context looks like in practice

$ bx "kubernetes ingress path rewrite rules" | jq '.grounding.generic[0]'
{
  "url": "https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/services-networking/ingress/",
  "title": "Ingress | Kubernetes",
  "snippets": [
    "An Ingress may be configured to give Services externally-reachable URLs...",
    "The rewrite-target annotation lets you rewrite the path before forwarding..."
  ]
}
 

Three things worth noting:

  • Pre-extracted clean text. No HTML tags, no navigation cruft, no cookie banners. The snippets[] array is ready to inject into a prompt.
  • Structured JSON on stdout. Easy to filter, cache, and reason over programmatically.
  • Search + extract in one call. In the built-in tools that would be web_search → pick a URL → web_fetch — two round-trips. bx context collapses that to one.

Why this matters — how I extended Lulu's tool surface when built-in tools fall short

Both the Goose in-cluster diagnostic pipeline (from the companion post) and Lulu — my OpenClaw personal-assistant agent — share the same three foundations:

  • A Kubernetes runtime. A pod with a PersistentVolumeClaim, restarted freely, state preserved.
  • LiteLLM in front of SAP AI Core as the model gateway. All model traffic — Claude tiers, Nova, embeddings — routes through it.
  • A clean separation between what belongs in the LLM proxy and what doesn't. Model requests: through LiteLLM. Everything else: local to the agent's workspace.

The LLM proxy is a model router, not a general-purpose tool router. Trying to squeeze search APIs into it inverts the abstraction. The right home for those is the workspace PVC, invoked via exec, with keys held in workspace-local env vars.

Practical implications for anyone running OpenClaw (or a similar agent) on Kubernetes with SAP AI Core underneath:

  • ✅The LLM proxy is not the place for search. Don't try to plumb Brave/Tavily/Exa API keys through LiteLLM.
  • ✅The workspace PVC is the place for out-of-band tools. Anything that needs an API key that isn't a model provider belongs as a workspace-scoped CLI, invoked via exec, with the key held locally.
  • ✅ TOOLS.md is the place to teach the agent about them. Skills are for shared patterns; TOOLS.md is for your environment and your PVC.
  • ✅The Kubernetes primitives you already have are enough. A PVC, a static binary, and one tools.exec.pathPrepend line.

Takeaways

  1. OpenClaw's built-in web search relies on DuckDuckGo by default. Check what provider is configured in your tools.web.search block — it may be silently returning nothing useful.
  2. You can't route search-vendor API keys through an LLM proxy. Structural limitation. Don't fight it.
  3. On Kubernetes, the workspace PVC + tools.exec.pathPrepend is a durable tool-extension pattern. Any static binary works. bx is one example.
  4. The agent doesn't discover local tools on its own. TOOLS.md is how you tell it.
  5. Document plan-specific gotchas explicitly. The OPTION_NOT_IN_PLAN note saves the agent one failed round-trip every time it considers bx answers.
  6. Same runtime, different agent. The Goose diagnostic pipeline and the OpenClaw personal-assistant agent share the same cluster, the same LiteLLM/SAP AI Core plumbing, and the same PVC-as-scratch-disk pattern. That's a platform doing its job.
  • ✅Survives container rebuilds.
  • ✅ Works with any model routed through LiteLLM.
  • ✅ Fully auditable — the CLI runs in your workspace, on your bill.
  • ✅ Generalises to any tool that ships as a static binary.

References

 

WhatWhere
OpenClawhttps://openclaw.ai/ · https://docs.openclaw.ai/
Brave Search CLI (bx)https://github.com/brave/brave-search-cli
Brave × OpenClaw guidehttps://brave.com/search/api/guides/use-with-openclaw/
Brave Search API dashboardhttps://api-dashboard.search.brave.com/
OpenClaw config: tools.exec.pathPrependdocs.openclaw.ai → Gateway → Configuration Reference
OpenClaw config: tools.web.search.providerdocs.openclaw.ai → Tools → Web Search
SAP AI Corehttps://help.sap.com/docs/sap-ai-core
LiteLLMhttps://docs.litellm.ai/
Companion post: Kyma Unplugged diagnosticshttps://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/autonomous-diagnostics-on-kyma-unplugged-goose-litellm-kro-sap-ai-core-in/ba-p/14435287

Stack referenced: OpenClaw · Brave Search API · bx CLI · Kyma Unplugged · LiteLLM · SAP AI Core · KRO

entry.title: Quick actions visibility when proxying
entry.content:
Hi SAP SF Community,

entry.title: Re: CRM Case partner table
entry.content: Hi,Logic to Fetch the Partner Number:- 1.Pass the case id in the EXT_KEY in the table-CRMS4_CASESEARCH and get the Case Guid 2.excecute the function module se37 -BAPI_CRMCASE_GETDETAIL 3.Pass the Case Guid in GUID field under Import Parameter tab and click on excute button 4.Result screen page will be display. 5.Go to the ELEMENT_ATTRIBUTE result page 6.Get the VALUE and ELEM_NO against the all PARTNER_FCT under the name column. 7.Back from the screen and go to ELEMENT RESULT page. 8.Check the element number which are fetch from ELEMENT_ATTRIBUTE with BOR_TYPE #BUS1006,fetch the value of BOR_KEY 9.Like this you can get the partner involved in case id.

entry.title: The GTT Route Simulator in SAP Build
entry.content:
About a year ago I published a blog post describing an integrated container scenario built with several SAP solutions—SAP Transportation Management, BN4L Freight Collaboration, and BN4L Global Track & Trace (GTT)—all running in the Industry Template Plus environment. To make that scenario feel a bit more realistic, I created a small SAP Build application that simulates an ocean-liner’s tracking information: it sends events to GTT so that an overseas voyage can be “played back” in just five minutes.

Using this tool—aptly named the GTT Route Simulator—the journey from the Port of Guangzhou to the Port of Rotterdam consists of:

  1. Proof-of-pickup, loading-start, loading-end and departure events are reported in Guangzhou.
  2. Location-update events are sent every few seconds while the vessel is at sea.
  3. Upon arrival in Rotterdam, arrival, unloading-start, unloading-end and proof-of-delivery events are reported.

When the simulator finishes, the shipment tracked event log in Global Track & Trace resembles a real shipment history, only compressed in time:

Building the Route Simulator with SAP Build

I put the first version together in a very short time—mainly to explore SAP Build’s capabilities. For anyone new to it, SAP Build is SAP’s low-code portfolio on SAP Business Technology Platform that lets business users and developers:

  • Create apps (SAP Build Apps)
    • Automate processes (SAP Build Process Automation)
    • Build digital workspaces (SAP Build Work Zone)

All of this is done with drag-and-drop tooling and enterprise-grade governance. The main program flow looks as follows:

The GTT Route Simulator is launched via a simple form where the user selects a scenario and a departure date/time. At the moment there are three configured scenarios:

  • Guangzhou → Rotterdam (standard ocean route)
    • Guangzhou → Rotterdam with a delay in the Suez Canal
    • Rotterdam → Saarbrücken (on-carriage - road leg)

Based on the selection, the main flow chooses a path and calls a subprocess that reports the loading events. Opening the sub process for the departure and loading events, demonstrates the steps that are executed:

  • A small JavaScript snippet prepares the data that must be embedded in the XML message sent to GTT.
  • A predefined action then transmits the XML payload to the GTT tenant.
  • The subprocess proceeds to the next event, and when finished, returns to the main flow.

The next subprocess handles periodic GPS updates to create the illusion of the vessel moving across the ocean. Once Rotterdam is reached, arrival and unloading events are posted.

Exception Handling: The Delay Scenario

Transport is a business of exceptions, so the second scenario is identical to the first until the vessel reaches the Suez Canal. There, the simulator injects a 5-day delay event that leads to the delayed status of the Freight Booking. This same delay scenario is currently used quite often for demonstrating the Transportation Reschedule Agent—a powerful AI-driven rescheduling feature, also developed within the IT Plus scope.

Final-Mile Scenario

The third scenario covers on-carriage from Rotterdam to a warehouse in Saarbrücken. I intentionally built in a three-hour delay because, in many demos, colleagues (or me) manually trigger the final transport and warehouse events. If some of those events are skipped, the simulator ensures the road freight order still completes so we don’t leave “open” documents cluttering the demo system—a little clean-desk policy for our landscape.

Why a Simulator?

The GTT Route Simulator does exactly what its name suggests—it mimics the tracking information normally provided by an LSP or directly by an ocean carrier. While SAP Build could ultimately retrieve real events and forward them to GTT, in production you would usually choose an integration platform such as SAP Cloud Integration (CPI) for that task. The simulator is therefore a lightweight, rapid-prototyping tool that accelerates demos and proofs of concept.

entry.title: Re: The planned orders are being generated even though th...
entry.content:
Hello

As already mentioned, there is file attached to this question. 

The most common cause for this issue is the rescheduling check. Either your order is outside the rescheduling horizon, or it is not included in the rescheduling check. If this is the case, you will probably see exception message 20 for you production order in MD04.

You can take a look into the following wiki, which provides more details about the rescheduling check.

Best regards,
Caetano

entry.title: Calling ABAP RFC Function Modules from SAP BTP Kyma with SAP Java Connector
entry.content:
Why I Wrote This

I recently wanted to build a small demo application that proves a simple point:

Can an application running on SAP BTP Kyma call an on-premise SAP system via RFC?

At first glance, this sounds like something that should be straightforward. SAP Java Connector exists, SAP Cloud Connector exists, Kyma has Connectivity Proxy, and SAP BTP has Destination service. Surely we just package sapjco3.jar, create a destination, and call STFC_CONNECTION, right?

Well — almost, but not quite.

While researching this, I found SAP Community questions such as “How can I connect my SAP BTP Kyma with my On-Premise system (ECC6 EHP7) via RFC”. The discussion captures the same uncertainty I ran into: Cloud Foundry examples exist, but Kyma is different because we build and run our own container images. There is no magic “built-in JCo” unless we explicitly bring the right runtime pieces (I was happy to see my own github repo referenced in the question - another indication that there is nothing out there for RFC and Kyma).

This blog documents the working path I ended up with.

The Goal

The goal was intentionally small:

  • Run a Java application in SAP BTP Kyma.
  • Resolve an RFC destination from SAP BTP Destination service.
  • Route RFC traffic through Kyma Connectivity Proxy.
  • Reach an on-premise SAP S/4HANA system via SAP Cloud Connector.
  • Execute STFC_CONNECTION.

The successful response looked like this:

{
  "ok": true,
  "destination": "InsightSuiteDestination",
  "function": "STFC_CONNECTION",
  "jcoVersion": "3.1.13.2 (2026-04-22) for SAP Business Technology Platform",
  "exports": {
    "ECHOTEXT": "Hello from Kyma",
    "RESPTEXT": "SAP R/3 Rel. 816   Sysid: S4H ... Logon_Data: 201/RFC_TESTUSER/E"
  }
}

That was the happy end. The rest of this post is about how to get there.

High-Level Architecture

The final request flow is:

HTTP client
  -> Kyma Istio route
  -> Java/Tomcat app in Kyma
  -> SAP JCo cloud runtime
  -> SAP BTP Destination service
  -> Kyma Connectivity Proxy RFC port 20001
  -> SAP Cloud Connector
  -> on-premise SAP system RFC gateway
  -> STFC_CONNECTION

For this demo, the relevant names were:

Kyma namespace:        jco-rfc-test
RFC destination:       InsightSuiteDestination
Cloud Connector ID:    sap-insight-suite
Virtual RFC host:      s4hana-2025japan
System number:         00
SAP client:            201
RFC function:          STFC_CONNECTION

Key Lesson 1: Plain JCo Is Not Enough

My first attempt was the obvious one: package SAP JCo into a Tomcat image and run a servlet.

That was enough to start the app, but not enough to make the BTP/Kyma on-premise path work. The plain runtime did not know how to resolve BTP destinations and route RFC through the Kyma Connectivity Proxy with the required proxy authorization.

The working setup needed the SAP JCo cloud/connectivity runtime libraries that are normally provided by SAP Java Buildpack.

The important runtime ingredients were:

com.sap.conn.jco.cloud-3.1.13.2.jar
com.sap.conn.jco.cloud.rt.cloud-3.1.13.2.jar
com.sap.core.connectivity.jco-5.0.7.jar
com.sap.core.connectivity.jco.cf-5.0.7.jar
com.sap.core.connectivity.cloud.destinations.tunnel-0.9.267.jar

In my case these came from:

xs-java-buildpack-2.66.0-offline-cnb-tomcat.tgz

I used the buildpack archive as a runtime overlay and copied its Tomcat libraries into my image. If you have access to the official builder, using pack build is the cleaner path.

The Minimal Java Code

The Java code itself is pleasantly small. The servlet resolves a destination and calls a function module:

JCoDestination destination = JCoDestinationManager.getDestination("InsightSuiteDestination");
JCoFunction function = destination.getRepository().getFunction("STFC_CONNECTION");

function.getImportParameterList().setValue("REQUTEXT", "Hello from Kyma");
function.execute(destination);

String echoText = function.getExportParameterList().getString("ECHOTEXT");
String responseText = function.getExportParameterList().getString("RESPTEXT");

The demo app exposes this through an HTTP endpoint:

/rfc?destination=InsightSuiteDestination&text=Hello%20from%20Kyma

Build Setup

The app is a WAR file running on Java 17 and Tomcat 10.1.

One important detail: SAP Java Buildpack 2.x uses the Jakarta servlet stack, so the application uses jakarta.servlet.*, not javax.servlet.*.

Relevant Maven setup:

<packaging>war</packaging>

<properties>
  <maven.compiler.release>17</maven.compiler.release>
</properties>

<dependencies>
  <dependency>
    <groupId>com.sap.conn.jco</groupId>
    <artifactId>sapjco3</artifactId>
    <version>3.1.13</version>
    <scope>system</scope>
    <systemPath>${project.basedir}/lib/sapjco3.jar</systemPath>
  </dependency>

  <dependency>
    <groupId>jakarta.servlet</groupId>
    <artifactId>jakarta.servlet-api</artifactId>
    <version>6.1.0</version>
    <scope>provided</scope>
  </dependency>
</dependencies>

Container Image

The working image used Tomcat 10.1 and copied the SAP buildpack runtime overlay:

FROM docker.io/library/maven:3.9-eclipse-temurin-17 AS build
WORKDIR /workspace
COPY pom.xml ./
COPY lib ./lib
COPY src ./src
RUN mvn -q -DskipTests package

FROM docker.io/library/tomcat:10.1-jre17-temurin
ENV LD_LIBRARY_PATH=/usr/local/tomcat/impl/linuxx86_64:/usr/local/tomcat/lib \
    JAVA_OPTS="-Djava.library.path=/usr/local/tomcat/impl/linuxx86_64:/usr/local/tomcat/lib"

COPY .tmp-buildpack/tomcat/bin/ /usr/local/tomcat/bin/
COPY .tmp-buildpack/tomcat/conf/ /usr/local/tomcat/conf/
COPY .tmp-buildpack/tomcat/lib/ /usr/local/tomcat/lib/
COPY .tmp-buildpack/tomcat/impl/ /usr/local/tomcat/impl/
COPY --from=build /workspace/target/jco-on-kyma-demo.war /usr/local/tomcat/webapps/ROOT.war
EXPOSE 8080

Before building, extract the buildpack archive:

mkdir -p .tmp-buildpack/tomcat
tar -xzf java-buildpack/xs-java-buildpack-2.66.0-offline-cnb-tomcat.tgz \
  -C .tmp-buildpack/tomcat

Then build and push the image to a registry Kyma can pull from.

Kyma Service Bindings

The application needs at least:

  • Destination service instance and binding
  • XSUAA service instance and binding
  • Connectivity Proxy config and secret available in the application namespace

Example BTP service resources:

apiVersion: services.cloud.sap.com/v1
kind: ServiceInstance
metadata:
  name: destination-jco
  namespace: jco-rfc-test
spec:
  serviceOfferingName: destination
  servicePlanName: lite
---
apiVersion: services.cloud.sap.com/v1
kind: ServiceBinding
metadata:
  name: destination-jco
  namespace: jco-rfc-test
spec:
  serviceInstanceName: destination-jco
---
apiVersion: services.cloud.sap.com/v1
kind: ServiceInstance
metadata:
  name: xsuaa-jco
  namespace: jco-rfc-test
spec:
  serviceOfferingName: xsuaa
  servicePlanName: application
---
apiVersion: services.cloud.sap.com/v1
kind: ServiceBinding
metadata:
  name: xsuaa-jco
  namespace: jco-rfc-test
spec:
  serviceInstanceName: xsuaa-jco

The SAP runtime expected the bindings as files. Injecting them only as environment variables was not sufficient in my setup.

- name: SERVICE_BINDING_ROOT
  value: "/etc/secrets/sapbtp"

And mount the binding secrets:

volumeMounts:
  - name: destination-binding
    mountPath: /etc/secrets/sapbtp/destination-jco
    readOnly: true
  - name: xsuaa-binding
    mountPath: /etc/secrets/sapbtp/xsuaa-jco
    readOnly: true

volumes:
  - name: destination-binding
    secret:
      secretName: destination-jco
  - name: xsuaa-binding
    secret:
      secretName: xsuaa-jco

Connectivity Proxy Environment

For RFC, the app uses Connectivity Proxy port 20001:

- name: ONPREMISE_PROXY_HOST
  valueFrom:
    configMapKeyRef:
      name: connectivity-proxy-info
      key: onpremise_proxy_host
- name: ONPREMISE_PROXY_RFC_PORT
  valueFrom:
    configMapKeyRef:
      name: connectivity-proxy-info
      key: onpremise_proxy_rfc_port
- name: ONPREMISE_PROXY_TENANT_MODE
  valueFrom:
    configMapKeyRef:
      name: connectivity-proxy-info
      key: onpremise_proxy_tenant_mode
- name: CP_SECRET_CONNECTIVITY_PROXY_KYMA_SYSTEM
  valueFrom:
    secretKeyRef:
      name: cp-secret-connectivity-proxy-kyma-system
      key: service_key

In this demo the proxy endpoint was:

connectivity-proxy.kyma-system.svc.cluster.local:20001

Creating the RFC Destination

This part is easy to miss.

The RFC destination is not a Kubernetes Destination custom resource. For this JCo flow, the destination must exist in SAP BTP Destination service, and JCo resolves it from there.

The working destination properties were:

Name=InsightSuiteDestination
Type=RFC
jco.client.ashost=s4hana-2025japan
jco.client.sysnr=00
jco.client.client=201
jco.client.user=RFC_TESTUSER
jco.client.passwd=<password>
jco.client.lang=EN
jco.destination.proxy_type=OnPremise
jco.client.cloud_connector_location_id=sap-insight-suite

The most important line for the Location ID was:

jco.client.cloud_connector_location_id=sap-insight-suite

Initially, I used only:

CloudConnectorLocationId=sap-insight-suite

That appeared as a raw/custom property but did not populate the RFC destination's Location ID field in SAP BTP Cockpit. The tunnel was opened as if the location ID was empty.

After adding jco.client.cloud_connector_location_id, Cockpit showed the Location ID correctly, and Connectivity Proxy opened the expected tunnel:

account:///.../sap-insight-suite

In the repository I created a small helper script to create the destination through the Destination service API:

export RFC_PASSWORD='<password>'

./scripts/upsert-rfc-destination.py \
  --namespace jco-rfc-test \
  --binding-secret destination-jco \
  --template destinations/InsightSuiteDestination.template.json \
  --delete-first

unset RFC_PASSWORD

Connectivity Proxy Authorization

Another issue I hit was proxy authorization. The Connectivity Proxy log showed:

Validation of token failed due to: Allowed client ids not set for region configuration id default

The fix was to configure the allowed client ID in the ConnectivityProxy custom resource:

CLIENTID=$(kubectl -n kyma-system get secret cp-secret-connectivity-proxy-kyma-system \
  -o jsonpath='{.data.service_key}' \
  | base64 -d \
  | python3 -c 'import json,sys; print(json.load(sys.stdin)["clientid"])')

python3 - <<PY > /tmp/cp-patch.json
import json
clientid = "$CLIENTID"
print(json.dumps({
  "spec": {
    "config": {
      "servers": {
        "proxy": {
          "authorization": {
            "oauth": {
              "allowedClientId": clientid
            }
          }
        }
      }
    }
  }
}))
PY

kubectl -n kyma-system patch connectivityproxy connectivity-proxy \
  --type merge \
  --patch-file /tmp/cp-patch.json

After this, the proxy accepted the token and opened the RFC tunnel.

How to Recognize Success

Application logs showed:

Connected to socket with {sap-insight-suite-RFC.connectivity,2}
RfcOpen(... SCCLOCATIONID=sap-insight-suite)=1
Executing function STFC_CONNECTION

Connectivity Proxy logs showed:

Will use tunnelId: account:///.../sap-insight-suite
Handshake with tunnel client completed successfully

And the endpoint returned:

{
  "ok": true,
  "exports": {
    "ECHOTEXT": "Hello from Kyma"
  }
}

Things I Would Watch Out For

Here are the stumbling blocks that cost the most time:

  1. Plain JCo is not enough
    You need the SAP JCo cloud/connectivity runtime, not only sapjco3.jar and libsapjco3.so.

  2. SAP Java Buildpack 2.x means Jakarta
    If you use the 2.x runtime libraries, use Java 17 and jakarta.servlet.*.

  3. Mount service bindings as files
    The runtime looked for service bindings under /etc/secrets/sapbtp.

  4. Use the RFC-specific Location ID property
    For RFC destinations, use jco.client.cloud_connector_location_id.

  5. Connectivity Proxy may need allowed client ID configuration
    If you see Allowed client ids not set, patch the ConnectivityProxy configuration.

  6. Reduce debug logging after troubleshooting
    JCo/connectivity debug logs can expose destination details and token payloads.

Final Thoughts

Once all pieces were in place, the actual RFC call was simple. The hard part was not the Java code; it was assembling the correct runtime and configuration around it.

The most important takeaway for me was this:

In Kyma, RFC via JCo is possible, but you need the SAP cloud JCo/connectivity runtime and a correctly maintained BTP RFC destination.

Hopefully this saves someone else a few debugging loops — especially around the Location ID and Connectivity Proxy authorization details.

Appendix: Minimal Checklist

  • [ ] Connectivity Proxy module is installed and RFC port 20001 is enabled.
  • [ ] SAP Cloud Connector is connected to the correct subaccount.
  • [ ] Cloud Connector exposes the virtual RFC host/system.
  • [ ] Destination service instance and binding exist in the app namespace.
  • [ ] XSUAA service instance and binding exist in the app namespace.
  • [ ] Service bindings are mounted under /etc/secrets/sapbtp.
  • [ ] App image includes SAP JCo cloud/connectivity runtime libraries.
  • [ ] RFC destination exists in BTP Destination service.
  • [ ] RFC destination has jco.client.cloud_connector_location_id if a Location ID is used.
  • [ ] Connectivity Proxy authorization allows the connectivity client ID.
  • [ ] STFC_CONNECTION works with the configured SAP user.

entry.title: [AGRIBUSINESS] SAP Learning Curriculum for Agribusiness
entry.content:
SAP Learning Curriculum for Agribusiness

The Agribusiness industry is undergoing rapid change — from digitalized farming operations to sustainable sourcing and data-driven decision-making. SAP Learning offers a dedicated curriculum for Agribusiness, covering SAP Intelligent Agriculture, SAP Agricultural Contract Management, and SAP Broker Reconciliation for Commodity Derivatives.

Below is an overview of the available Agribusiness learning content, with links to SAP Learning.

Suggested Learning Path

Step Course Level Purpose

1IAG010 – Introducing the Agribusiness IndustryBeginnerIndustry foundation
2ISAG101 – Positioning the Agribusiness Industry (Partner)BeginnerSales & presales enablement
3IAG71 – Exploring SAP Intelligent AgricultureBeginnerSolution overview
4IAG72 – Using SAP Intelligent AgricultureBeginnerHands-on navigation
5IAG73 – Implementing SAP Intelligent Agriculture – IntegrationBeginnerImplementation & APIs
6IAG310 – Exploring SAP Agricultural Contract ManagementIntermediateTrading & origination

Courses

 

Foundation

IAG010 – Introducing the Agribusiness Industry
Beginner · 2 hr+ · Leads to Achievement

Entry-level introduction to the Agribusiness industry and SAP's portfolio for Agribusiness.

Covers:

  • Agribusiness value chain and key industry-specific business processes
  • Current trends and challenges in the sector
  • Overview of SAP solutions supporting Agribusiness operations

Designed for:

  • Learners new to the Agribusiness industry
  • Consultants and business users looking for industry context
  • Anyone starting a learning journey in SAP for Agribusiness

Typically the starting point before moving into solution-specific courses.

🔗IAG010 – Introducing the Agribusiness Industry

Sales & Presales (Partner)

ISAG101 – Positioning the Agribusiness Industry
Beginner · 1 hr 27 min · 3 Units · Leads to Achievement · Available to SAP employees and partners

Sales and presales enablement course for positioning SAP's Agribusiness portfolio in customer interactions.

Covers:

  • SAP's go-to-market priorities and value proposition for Agribusiness in 2026
  • Key factors and customer needs in Agribusiness
  • SAP reference stories relevant to Agribusiness
  • 2026 Sales Plays for Agribusiness
  • SAP Business AI for Agribusiness — value, benefits, and key capabilities
  • Key product roadmap and demonstration techniques for customer interactions

Designed for:

  • SAP and partner sales and presales roles
  • Anyone preparing for customer-facing Agribusiness engagements

Prerequisite: Introducing the Agribusiness Industry (IAG010)

🔗ISAG101 – Positioning the Agribusiness Industry

SAP Intelligent Agriculture

IAG71 – Exploring SAP Intelligent Agriculture
Beginner · 17 min · Leads to Achievement

Short introduction to SAP Intelligent Agriculture as a native cloud solution.

Covers:

  • Types of customers and business areas relevant to the solution
  • Main capabilities and benefits of SAP Intelligent Agriculture
  • Overview of how data science and machine learning support farming decisions

Designed for:

  • Anyone evaluating or starting with SAP Intelligent Agriculture
  • Presales, sales, and customer success roles

🔗IAG71 – Exploring SAP Intelligent Agriculture

IAG72 – Using SAP Intelligent Agriculture
Beginner · 1 hr+ · Leads to Achievement

Practical course for working with the SAP Intelligent Agriculture solution.

Covers:

  • Accessing and navigating SAP Intelligent Agriculture
  • Using the range of applications available in the solution
  • Applying data science and machine learning capabilities to farming operations

Designed for:

  • End users and consultants working day-to-day with the system
  • Agribusiness and consumer goods company project teams

Beginner · 1 hr+ · Leads to Achievement

  • Understanding the farm data model

Designed for:

  • Technical project team members responsible for integration

SAP Agricultural Contract Management

Intermediate · 6 hr+

Covers:

  • Industry concepts in Agricultural Origination and Trading
  • Main components, supported processes, and master data in SAP Agricultural Contract Management
  • Contracts, inventory handling, and fee frameworks
  • Prepayments, pricing, load data capture, settlement, and revenue recognition

entry.title: Re: Function module RSDMD_API_DELETE does not delete
entry.content: Hello Roland, I have the solution now: the parameter i_flg_delete_all (for fm RSDMD_API_DELETE) must be passed with the value false or omitted entirely (default is false). You don't necessarily have to understand the logic behind this when you simply want to delete everything related to the characteristic value. Best regards, Britta

entry.title: Mass clearing of GL account open items
entry.content:
Hi,

We are currently performing archiving in our SAP system (ECC). During archiving, we realized that there are huge number of GL open items (62 million open item records per fiscal year per GL account) in older years (like 2012,2013...)

Type of GL account: Balance Sheet account. It is Open item Managed account but not a reconciliation account.

As per the SAP standard archiving process, the open items cannot be archived. The line item has to cleared to be able to archive it. Business has also accepted to clear these open items for older years by any means. We tried below ways to clear but could not be successful due to some challenges.

  • We tried F.13 automatic clearing program for clearing. But the primary rule for F.13 is that debit and credit balances must match. In our case, the majority of open items debit and credit balance don't match at the Profit Centre level, which prevents the system from clearing. 
  • We have identified scenarios where a finance document contains multiple Profit Centers. Since F.13 operates on a total balance basis rather than at the individual line-item level, it fails to recognize these complex offsets.  
  • We have also discovered that some items are already marked as "cleared" in the system. This has caused an imbalance between the actual debit and credit totals, further complicating the reconciliation process.
  • We also tried Standard SAP Program RFVD_CHK_CHANGE_CLEARING to update the clearing information and mark as cleared. But this program is not replicating the documents to CFIN S4 system. 

    Do we have any program to clear the open item in mass even if the debit and credit balance don't match. Or any enhancement idea to clear this GL account. Appreciate your response.

entry.title: SAP S/4HANA Custom ABAP CDS View to Datasphere: Replication Flow Setup, Status & Delta Processing
entry.content:
Introduction: 

This technical guide explains how to replicate data from a custom ABAP CDS view in SAP S/4HANA into SAP Datasphere using Replication Flows with Change Data Capture (CDC). It covers the concepts, the end-to-end setup, how deltas are captured, the related jobs and system tables, and what to expect during initial and delta runs.
You’ll learn:
  • How RMS, APE, and CDC work together
  • How to prepare a CDC-enabled custom CDS view
  • How to configure a Replication Flow in Datasphere for Initial only and Initial + Delta loads
  • What artifacts (logging and buffer tables, jobs) are created in S/4HANA and how to monitor them
  • How to validate inserts, updates, and deletes end-to-end
  • SAP Datasphere local table, Internal delta capture columns (Change Date or Change Type).

Basics:

  • Replication Management Service (RMS): Orchestrates change data capture lifecycles, creates and monitors master/subscriber logging tables, and runs real-time jobs for observing and pushing deltas. Manages the end-to-end reliability of triggers and logging.
  • ABAP Pipeline Engine (APE): An ABAP-based runtime used by Datasphere to extract from ABAP sources (S/4HANA tables or CDC-enabled CDS views). Acts as the “consumer” in CDC terms for Replication Flows. Supporting Initial Only, Delta Only & Initial and Delta options.
  • Change Data Capture (CDC): Uses database triggers to record INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE changes on base tables that back an ABAP CDS view.Captured changes flow from: Base table triggers → Master Logging Table → Subscriber Logging Table → Buffer Table → Replication Flow into Datasphere target.

Prerequisites and Preparation:

Authorizations:
  • ABAP development access to create a CDS view
  • Access to SM37, DHADM, DHCDCMON, DHRDBMON for monitoring
  • Permissions in SAP Datasphere to create and run Replication Flows
System readiness:
  • RFC/ODP or ABAP connection from Datasphere to S/4HANA (as per your Datasphere connection setup)
  • Sufficient background work processes for real-time jobs
SAP S/4HANA RMS job settings, scheudling and operational tuning:

Standard jobs (auto-created/managed, visible in SM37; commonly run under DDIC)

  • /1DH/CDC_HEALTH_CHECK: Periodic CDC health verification.
  • SAP_DHCDC_DISPATCHER: Dispatcher for CDC orchestration.
  • /1DH/OBSERVE_LOGTAB: Moves changes from Master Logging Table to Subscriber Logging Table. Default reschedule interval ~1 hour.
  • /1DH/PUSH_CDS_DELTA (aka /1DH/CDS_PUSH_DELTA): Joins subscriber logs with base table and writes Buffer Table. Default reschedule interval ~6 hours.

Tuning guidance:

  • Start with defaults, then shorten intervals if your delta SLAs require it and your system resources allow.
  • Coordinate with Basis to adjust job class/priority and periodicity to avoid long-run job lists and ensure timely delta movement.
  • Monitor DHCDCMON (log table depth) and DHRDBMON (buffer latency). If backlogs build, increase job frequency and/or background work processes.

Connectivity:

  • ABAP or SAP S/4HANA Connection between SAP Datasphere and SAP S/4HANA.
  • SAP Datasphere + SAP S/4HANA: Your Guide to Seamless Data Integration.
  • SAP Cloud Connector for SAP S/4HANA (Private/ On-prem).
Design recommendations: Prefer SAP standard released CDS views or SAP data products where possible for lifecycle stability. If not then create custom ABAP CDS view/entity, data products instead of using direct ABAP tables as context information is lost and significant effort is required to reverse-engineer the logic. 
  • SAP Datasphere: Extract, Transform & Load (ETL) - Best Practices

1. Create a CDC‑Enabled Custom CDS View

The example below shows a simplified custom ABAP CDS view Z_SALESORDER on top of a custom base table ZVBAK. It enables analytical usage and data extraction, and configures CDC mapping so deltas are captured.

Sample reference code:

@AbapCatalog.sqlViewName: 'ZISDSALESORDER'
@AbapCatalog.compiler.compareFilter: true
@AbapCatalog.preserveKey: true
@EndUserText.label: 'Z Sales Order'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
@ObjectModel: {
   compositionRoot:   true,
   representativeKey: 'SalesOrder',
   usageType: {
     dataClass:      #TRANSACTIONAL,
     serviceQuality: #B,
     sizeCategory:   #L
   },
   supportedCapabilities: [ #ANALYTICAL_DIMENSION, #CDS_MODELING_ASSOCIATION_TARGET, #SQL_DATA_SOURCE, #CDS_MODELING_DATA_SOURCE, #EXTRACTION_DATA_SOURCE ],
   modelingPattern: [ #ANALYTICAL_DIMENSION ]
}
@ObjectModel.sapObjectNodeType.name: 'SalesOrder'

@Analytics: {
    dataExtraction: {
        enabled: true,
        delta.changeDataCapture: {
            mapping:[
                {
                    table: 'zvbak', role: #MAIN,
                    viewElement: ['SalesOrder'],
                    tableElement: ['vbeln']  }
            ]
        }
    }
 }
define view Z_SALESORDER as select from zvbak
//Association
  association [0..*]...{<Columns>};

For testing, I developed a custom ABAP program to perform CREATE, UPDATE, and DELETE operations on the ZVBAK custom table and validate end‑to‑end propagation. I initially inserted 10 records.

Use transaction SE16N on the CDS SQL view ZISDSALESORDER to view the record count.

2. Create a Replication Flow in SAP Datasphere.

Replication Flows are the recommended mechanism for high‑volume replication into SAP Datasphere and supported targets.

2.1 Note: If you choose Initial Only mode, the target will have the same columns as the source (130 columns in this case).

2.2 When using the Initial and Delta mode, SAP Datasphere adds two internal columns (Change_Type and Change_Date) to the Z_SALESORDER local table. The following Change_Type values are typically observed in ABAP CDS replication. Replication Flow Scenarios with Different Load Types.

OperationChange_Type
Initial Load L
Pure Insert I
Delta Load (UPSERT: insert if not exists, else update by key )A 
Delta Load (DELETE)D 

Note: The delta capture table is an internal SAP Datasphere object whose structure may change at any time and is not released for external access.It is only used by SAP Datasphere internal applications (for example, Transformation Flows, Replication Flows etc.)Using the internal delta capture columns (Change_Date or Change_Type) or their content for any external delta replication outside Premium Outbound Integration is not permitted. 

3725855 - Delta Columns CHANGE_TYPE and CHANGE_DATE not visible in Data Viewer or Data Products for Local Tables in HDLF Spaces in SAP Datasphere

3634750 - Unexpected behavior with delta enabled Datasphere local table and models using the same feature

2.3  The Replication Flow’s delta frequency is configured for every 5 minutes (default: 1 hour). You can modify this as required. Using 0 hours and 0 minutes switches to near real‑time.

 

3. Execute the Replication Flow for the first time to perform the Initial Load

3.1 In DHADM, you can see that a new subscriber and buffer table are created in SAP S/4HANA for CDS Z_SALESORDER (check via T‑Codes DHADM and DHRDBMON). This occurs when you run the Replication Flow for the first time. Subscriber ID: C30227C32B841FE19EF81806E972D0BE; Producer Type: CDC; Consumer Type: APE (ABAP Pipeline Engine).

3.2 Master Loggining Table /1DH/ML000000024 and Subscriber Logging table /1DH/SL000000024 got created and can also be monitored in the Transaction DHCDCMON.

Master Logging Table: /1DH/ML<XXXXXXXXX>, It captures delta records from triggers as the single source of truth.

  • Structure: Includes the base table’s key fields and a sequence key (/1DH/SEQUENCE).
  • Quantity: One Master Logging Table per CDC mapping in the CDS view. For example, two CDC mappings generate two Master Logging Tables.

Subscriber Logging Table: /1DH/SL<XXXXXXXXX>, It is populated by the real‑time job /1DH/OBSERVE_LOGTAB when the Master Logging Table has records and Mirrors the Master Logging Table but uses the base table’s key fields.

3.3 Resilient Data Buffer Table: /1DH/RDB0000HK: /1DH/RDB<XXXXX>, It is a transient staging framework / buffer table in the ABAP system that acts as a temporary staging area between S/4HANA and SAP Datasphere, ensuring deltas are safely staged, acknowledged, and only deleted after successful delivery. You can monitor them via transactions DHRDBMON (S/4HANA) or LTRDBMON (DMIS-based systems).

  • For each CDS view + replication flow combination, the ABAP Pipeline Engine/APRS creates one or more RDB tables as buffer objects.  One CDS view + one replication flow → one RDB table. Same CDS view + second replication flow → another RDB table for that flow.
  • The final structure matching the CDS view; populated by the real‑time job /1DH/CDS_PUSH_DELTA and Mirrors the CDS view fields; /1DH/SEQUENCE serves as the key.
  • /1DH/CDS_PUSH_DELTA joins the Subscriber Logging Table with the base table to fill the Buffer Table, ensuring only delta records are forwarded to the target.

3.4 As shown below, the Initial Load has completed, and the Replication Flow is retrying deltas every 5 minutes as configured.

 

3.5 In SAP Datasphere, preview the Z_SALESORDER table; the Change_Type = 'L' after initial load is completed and Change_Date was updated when the records were inserted.

 

4. Execute the Replication Flow: Delta Load with INSERT, UPDATE, MODIFY and DELETE Scenarios

4.1 Using the ABAP program, INSERT 2 new sales orders into ZVBAK. In SE16N (ZISDSALESORDER), you’ll see 12 records, and DHRDBMON shows 2 new entries as Current records.

 

 

Buffer Table /1DH/RDB0000HK-/1DH/OPERATION value is U means “UPSERT” (used for both INSERT and UPDATE operations in the RDB buffer)

The Replication Flow status switches from “Retrying” to “Delta Running” whenever records are present in the RDB and the delta load frequency interval elapses.

You’ll notice that the two new orders (5011, 5012) have Change_Type = “A” (upsert: insert if not present, or update by primary key if it exists), whereas “L” denotes the initial load (records loaded during the first full load).

 

4.2 UPDATE 3 existing sales order: 5004, 5005,5006.

You’ll notice that for orders 5004, 5005, and 5006, Change_Type changed from “L” (Initial Load) to “A” (Upsert).

4.3 DELETE three existing orders: 5006, 5007, and 5008.

In the buffer table /1DH/RDB0000HK, the /1DH/OPERATION value is “D” for DELETE operations

In the SAP Datasphere local table Z_SALESORDER, deleted records appear with Change_Type = “D.” On the source side (SAP S/4HANA table ZVBAK and the CDS view Z_SALESORDER), those records were removed from the database. This confirms that Replication Flows with CDC in SAP Datasphere capture all changes, CREATE, UPDATE, and DELETE.

5.High‑Level Flow for ABAP CDS View Replication into SAP Datasphere

On the first execution of a Replication Flow, the system creates the subscriber tables, RDB buffer tables, and logging tables. These tables are generated per object + replication flow combination.

The Initial Load transfers data from the source CDS view to SAP Datasphere, and the corresponding Change_Type = Lin the Datasphere local table.

For delta loads, CDC database triggers capture CRUD changes on the base table and write them to the Master Logging Table.

  • Master Logging Table: Stores delta records with /1DH/SEQUENCE as the key.
  • Observer Job ( /1DH/OBSERVE_LOGTAB ) Moves entries from the Master Logging Table to the Subscriber Logging Table.
  • Subscriber Logging Table: Holds delta records keyed by the base table’s keys.
  • Push Delta Job ( /1DH/CDS_PUSH_DELTA ) Joins subscriber logs with the base table to prepare records for extraction.
  • Buffer Table: Provides the final CDS-aligned structure for Datasphere to ingest into the target defined in the Replication Flow.

The Replication Flow status switches from “Retrying” to “Delta Running” whenever records are present in the RDB and the delta load interval has elapsed.

After the initial load, for delta processing:

  • Create/Update operations are represented as Change_Type = A
  • Delete operations are represented as Change_Type = D

 

Conclusion:

Replication Flows with CDC provide a reliable way to move CDS-based data from SAP S/4HANA into SAP Datasphere with clear handling for initial loads, updates, inserts, and deletes. During the first run, the full dataset is loaded into the target, and during delta processing the runtime automatically captures source changes through triggers, logging tables, and the resilient data buffer before delivering them to Datasphere. In the target local table, the Change_Type values help validate the lifecycle of each record: L for initial load, A for upsert-style delta changes, and D for deletions. This makes it easier to monitor data movement end to end and confirm that the source and target remain synchronized.

 

References:

  1. https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_DATASPHERE 
  2. https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/replication-flow-blog-series-part-1-overvi... 
  3. https://pages.community.sap.com/topics/datasphere/best-practices-troubleshooting 
  4. https://help.sap.com/docs/SUPPORT_CONTENT/datasphere/4428871866.html 

Happy Learning!😊

entry.title: Re: From SEGW to @OData.publish: Expose Your CDS View as OData in S/4HANA
entry.content:
Yes - There are 3 ways to develop OData service:

  1. @odata.publish: true annotation

    • This method automatically creates OData services from CDS views using a simple annotation.
    • It is easy and fast, mainly good for read-only or simple analytical services.
    • Not meant for complex business logic or transactional apps.

  2. SEGW (Service Builder) with BOPF

    • This is the classic way to build OData services with the Service Builder tool and optionally using BOPF for business logic.
    • Gives you full control and flexibility, good if you need special ABAP code or legacy support.
    • More manual work compared to annotation-driven methods.

  3. RESTful ABAP Programming Model (RAP)

    • The newest and recommended way for building modern transactional OData services and Fiori apps.
    • Uses CDS views with behavior definitions and manages service binding without SEGW.
    • Provides better reusability, cloud readiness, and fits with newer SAP S/4HANA releases.
    • Ideal if you want future-proof transactional apps and is part of ABAP Cloud development.

How to choose?

  • Use @odata.publish for quick read-only services or analytics.
  • Use SEGW+BOPF for custom or complex legacy projects requiring classic ABAP.
  • Use RAP for new transactional apps and future-ready developments, especially in newer SAP versions.

The decision depends on your project needs like whether the app is read-only or needs complex transactions, special ABAP logic, or the SAP system version you use.

I will be covering them in my upcoming blogs 🙂

entry.title: Request for information for SAP Intgeration Suite
entry.content:
Hi, we are using SAP Integration Suite to devlopp mutilple Flow and we have some questions :

Concurrency & Threading

 

What is the maximum number of concurrent iFlow executions (worker threads) allowed per tenant on the Integration Suite Starter/Edge plan? Is this configurable or fixed?

When multiple instances of the same iFlow run in parallel, are they queued, rejected, or executed with degraded performance? What is the exact queuing/throttling behavior?

Does concurrent execution of the same iFlow share any runtime resources (JVM heap, thread pool, Camel routes) that could cause performance degradation as concurrency increases?

 

HTTP/Message Processing Timeouts

 

What is the maximum synchronous HTTP request processing timeout enforced by the Integration Suite runtime (Cloud Foundry router + CPI runtime) for a single message exchange? Is this configurable at the iFlow, tenant, or platform level?

Is there a difference in timeout behavior between the HTTPS Sender adapter and other trigger types (e.g., timer-based, JMS-based)?

If a message exceeds the timeout mid-processing, is there a way to receive a partial-comp

entry.title: Re: Is there an audit report that can be used to review S...
entry.content: Hi Tiffaney, there isn't a self-service audit report available directly in SuccessFactors for mobile app usage at this time. This is a known gap we're actively working to address. In the meantime, your CSM can pull a Mobile Usage Report on your behalf through an internal dashboard. The report covers unique users by feature for a given month and can be shared with you as an XLSX extract. I'd recommend reaching out to your CSM and requesting this report. Just let them know the company and the time period you're interested in.

entry.title: Re: To use performance (MBO) rating as a direct payout % for individual section
entry.content: The first thing I see is that the rating is set as 0.0. So then the guideline is 0%-0%. If you have only a calculated amount (no discretion), I would build out the 1 - 200 % guideline with a default value equal to the achievement and let system calculate. So the system knows a rating of 135 will produce a value of 135% of the target. So make sure your PM template is correctly connected to the Var Pay Template and the correct rating is being used. If you get a rating populated, the payout should calculate.

entry.title: Re: To use performance (MBO) rating as a direct payout %...
entry.content:
@petre_manvelishvili It is not possible to use one of the calculated rating score from Performance as default value for individual payout % field on the summary level, you must use a guideline table to populate that value. It is a fairly easily guideline table however as long as you are able to bring over the calculated rating from performance summary section into the pmRating standard field of the VarPay template. 

"VarPay template --> set bonus calculation --> rating Source = Performance Management, Rating = Percent Done (Complete) as rating (goals Section)" is for this design in the goal section directly.

I am about 90% sure this is part of the Variable Pay certification training and questions which we recommend you pass asap.

entry.title: Re: customer invoices are not visible in E-document cockp...
entry.content:
Hi Jerrysam,

Short answer: The framework and connectivity are fine (Poland proves that) — Germany simply isn't triggering eDocument creation. That is almost always because the German country/process is not activated, the German company code isn't the one posting, or the billing-document-to-eDocument-type mapping for Germany is missing. Connectivity/value-mapping problems would still create an eDocument that then errors — they would not make it invisible.

Does "Poland works, Germany doesn't" mean something is broken?

Not really. Poland flowing end-to-end confirms the eDocument framework, the connection to SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, cloud edition, and your apps all work. The gap is Germany-specific setup only. This is the exact scenario in SAP KBA 3683911 (Public Cloud, "eDocument not appearing in eDocument Cockpit"). Reference: https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3683911

Is Germany activated in Document and Reporting Compliance Features?

Activation is per country/region. If only Poland was added, German documents never generate an eDocument. Check the config activity Activate Document and Reporting Compliance Features and confirm the DE country/region key (or "All Countries/Regions") is present and active.

Reference: https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0c676015e0414931bdc9ebacd6eb2dff/f18f5271e84a43228c1355c973932407.html

Is the German company code the one posting the billing document?

In Public Cloud the process is determined by the company code's country, so a German invoice must post from a DE company code. A German-formatted invoice raised under a non-DE company code will not create a German eDocument. Reference (KBA 3604801, German e-invoices / company-code scenario, Public Cloud): https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3604801

Now validate the full German setup against SAP's Public Cloud guide

Do this end-to-end. The blog Electronic Invoicing in SAP S/4HANA Cloud, public edition lists every mandatory activity for Germany and shows a screenshot of each one — use it as your checklist. Reference (with screenshots per step): https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-blog-posts-by-sap/electronic-invoicing-in-sap-s-4hana-cloud-public-edition/ba-p/13578774

Config section — General Settings for eDocument (governs whether an eDocument is created — this is why nothing appears):

  • Activating Source Type Documents for Company Code (Mandatory) — the switch that says "create eDocuments for this company code + source". If not set for your DE company code, the cockpit stays empty.
  • Assigning eDocument Type to Accounting Document Type (Mandatory).
  • Activate Additional eDocument Instance Creation.

Config section — Settings for PEPPOL (governs valid content + submission — this is where German setups usually fail):

  • Assign Party ID Types to Business Partners (Mandatory).
  • Assign Party ID Types to ID Types (Mandatory).
  • Assign Party ID Types to Companies (Mandatory).
  • Edit tax information for company codes (ID 105675) → VAT number for company code (Mandatory).
  • Maintain additional parameters (ID 102739) → tax number, non-VAT, for company code (Mandatory).

Party ID assignment — treat as the prime suspect

Check all three Party ID activities together. For PEPPOL/Germany the three assignments above must be consistent, and the business partner must carry the correct party ID (and, for B2G/public authorities, a Leitweg ID on the Identification tab of the business partner). A wrong or missing Party ID is the classic reason a German eDocument either fails or won't submit. Especially make sure all relevant business parties are listed in Assign Party ID Types to Business Partners. You really need to keep the list up to date.

Value mappings (/EDUBL namespace)

Maintain them per tenant in the Configure Value Mapping app; e.g. INVOICE_TYPE_CODE_SD maps internal document types to the PEPPOL values used in Germany. Missing mappings usually produce an eDocument that then errors in the application log rather than a blank cockpit.

If an eDocument is created but you still don't see it clean

Then it's content/connectivity, not creation. Open both logs in eDocument Cockpit / Manage Electronic Documents: application log (content) and interface log (connectivity to DRC cloud edition / PEPPOL). Also confirm the DRC cloud edition initial setup on BTP and the PEPPOL Exchange integration are in place. Reference SAP Help

Constraints to verify:

  • German outbound runs via PEPPOL (XRechnung/UBL) or e-mail (ZUGFeRD) — confirm the DRC/PEPPOL communication setup is scoped for DE, not only PL.
  • Value mappings and the SAP DRC cloud edition connection must exist separately in each tenant (test and production).
  • German SAP Notes to keep handy: Overview 2690949, Troubleshooting 2926868.

Summary

Since Poland works, treat this purely as incomplete German config. Go top-down: (1) DE active in Activate DRC Features, (2) invoice posts from a DE company code, (3) Activate Source Type Documents for Company Code + Assign eDocument Type — these three fix the empty cockpit. Then validate the PEPPOL section, especially the three Party ID assignments (plus VAT/Leitweg and /EDUBL value mappings), which is where German documents typically break once they start being created. The linked blog shows a screenshot for each of these activities.

Sources:

  • Electronic Invoicing in SAP S/4HANA Cloud, public edition — full German config with screenshots
  • KBA 3683911 – Germany eInvoice: eDocument not appearing in eDocument Cockpit
  • KBA 3604801 – German electronic invoices in Public Cloud
  • Activating Document and Reporting Compliance Features
  • Germany – Document and Reporting Compliance (Public Cloud)

Hope it helps.

Best regards,

Michal

entry.title: Re: Eligible employee not getting added to Variable Pay form
entry.content: You are on the right track. You need to check the hierarchy and make sure the manager is correct. I would try to manually add the user to the form. The system will indicate if the employee is not considered to be eligible. Double check your eligibility rules if the system kicks back that the employee is not eligible. If the employee can be added manually, the problem is most likely hierarchy related and you need to make sure the manager relationship is correct as of the effective date.

entry.title: Re: How to optimise query performance in sap datasphere i...
entry.content:
Hi,

Without persistency the performance optimization may be a challenge. First check if a simple view directly from SNOW is fast enough, this way you ensure the connection is OK and working for virtualization. 

If the basic check is fast enough, then go for the Views self. Best approach is the HANA PlanViz. You can get it via DB Access and then analyze in Eclipse / HANA Studio. PlanViz is like Exec Plan, just far more powerful. You can find a lot of blogs how to analyze PlanViz (just enable the critical path and complexity of PlanViz will be reduced). 

Approach without persistency is basically as in any DB performance analyzes. 

Hope this helps. Again without persistency, it may be a challenge.

Cheers, Martin

entry.title: Re: Direct Email out of Customer Invoices in Financial Ac...
entry.content:
Short answer: SAP21 is an on‑demand correspondence — it is not e‑mailed automatically when the FI invoice is posted, and API‑posted invoices are not even eligible for it until you add the BAdI from note 3757894 as you mentioned. So the "automatic e‑mail on creation" you describe is not achievable with standard SAP21; the note enables eligibility, not automation.

"Please advise how to set up the automatic e‑mail output of SAP21 for invoices created via API"

No supported — not as automatic output. Standard SAP21 is issued on demand from the Create Correspondence app (or Manage Customer Line Items → Create Correspondence). There is no event that emits SAP21 automatically when the accounting document is posted, and no mass/scheduled job for it. Reference: Printing of Customer Invoices in FI; and still open influence request 331512. Please vote there to speed up its delivery.

Note: You can configure the e‑mail channel and a fixed dedicated recipient in Output Parameter Determination, but the actual issuing of the PDF stays a manual/on‑demand step. Reference: Configuring the Output of FI Customer Invoices.

"Probably we have to implement note 3757894"

Yes — but only to make your API documents eligible, not to automate sending. Standard SAP21 only accepts documents posted with BKPF‑TCODE = FB70 / FBDC_P020 / FBDC_P021 (i.e. Create Outgoing Invoices or Upload Customer Open Items in public cloud); API/interface‑posted documents fail this origin check and would raise error F0744 if you tried to preview them. Note 3757894 provides BAdI FICUS_INV_ADJUST_FILTER (Custom Logic app, F6957), where you set PRINT_ELIGIBLE = X to allow SAP21 for your API‑posted invoices. Reference: KBA 3757894; KBA 3557212.

Important (BAdI behavior): with your own implementation, the "has a customer line item" and "sales‑related (BSEG‑XUMSW = X)" checks still run — only the TCODE check is dropped (reimplement it if you want to keep it). So your API postings must carry a sales‑related customer line.

"Our understanding is that the e‑mail output is generated automatically"

No — for neither manually created nor API‑created documents. SAP21 is always a correspondence you issue on demand; it is not generated automatically at posting time. The BAdI removes the origin restriction for API documents but adds no automation. 

Final notes:

  • Confirm your API sets a sales‑related customer line (BSEG‑XUMSW = X); otherwise BAdI check (2) still blocks generation.
  • Maintain the dedicated address via the Email Recipient determination step (fixed), not from BP master data.
  • There is no standard trigger/API to auto‑issue SAP21 at posting; if fully automatic dispatch is mandatory, evaluate SD billing output (which does e‑mail automatically on billing‑document creation) or vote and regularly check back influence request 331512.
  • In Public Cloud, "implementing" note 3757894 means building this BAdI via Custom Logic (F6957) — there is no SNOTE.

Summary

Configure the SAP21 e‑mail output (dedicated recipient) and implement BAdI FICUS_INV_ADJUST_FILTER so your API‑posted invoices become eligible — but plan for a manual/on‑demand issuing step in Create Correspondence, because standard SAP21 does not auto‑e‑mail at posting and cannot be mass‑scheduled today.

Hope it helps.

Best Regards,

Michal
